content.text: is the initial API call to Claude that includes the
parameter, defining which functions are available for Claude to call. This request tells Claude what operations it can perform and when to use them.
: "You send a request with tools defined → Claude responds with tool_use blocks (stop_reason: 'tool_use') → your application executes → feed results back → Claude gives final answer".
: Claude can only generate text → no external actions
: Claude knows available tools → can call them when needed → performs real-world actions
For your distributed systems work at Box Hill
Use direct response for trivial tasks

Step 1: Initial Request → User message + tools defined
Step 2: Tool Request → Claude outputs tool_use blocks (stop_reason: "tool_use")
Step 3: Data Retrieval → Your app executes tools and collects results
Step 4: Final Response → Send tool_result back + Claude synthesizes answer

// With default tool_choice: { type: "auto" }
Claude decides on each turn whether to call a tool or respond directly.

It calls a tool when:
1. The request maps to that tool's described capability
2. AND the answer isn't already in context

It responds directly for:
- Stable knowledge (e.g., "What's the capital of France?")
- Creative tasks (e.g., writing, poetry)
- Conversational turns (e.g., chat)
: Claude intelligently decides when tools are needed.
: Forces tool call instead of nudging.
Handle Multiple Tool Calls in Parallel
Tool use request = initial API call with tools parameter
: Request → Tool Request → Data Retrieval → Final Response
→ Claude decides when to use tools
→ "Use tools to investigate" increases tool use
→ Forces tool call instead of nudge
→ Control flow signal for agent logic
→ Tool definitions = input tokens, keep focused
For your Box Hill distributed systems work, I'd implement
+ system prompt "Use the tools to investigate before responding" +
. This gives you intelligent tool selection while ensuring Claude always checks external data before responding.
When working with tool use request, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with tool use request. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of tool use request scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
